query: effect of desflurane on left ventricular function during aortic surgery

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia Inhalation Agents and Their Cardiovascular Effects -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Inhaled anesthetic agents are widely used in clinical practice to induce and maintain general anesthesia during surgical procedures. Common agents include halothane, sevoflurane, desflurane, isoflurane, enflurane, and nitrous oxide, each with distinct pharmacodynamic and pharmacokinetic properties. These agents exert dose-dependent cardiovascular effects, such as fluctuations in systemic vascular resistance, myocardial contractility, and mean arterial pressure, which may contribute to hemodynamic instability, particularly in patients with preexisting cardiovascular disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Inhalational Anesthetic -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Isoflurane, sevoflurane, and desflurane all decrease systemic blood pressure by decreasing systemic vascular resistance. For the most part, these agents preserve cardiac output, but cardiac depression can be seen if combined with other IV agents or in patients with acute cardiogenic shock.  Desflurane has been known to cause hypertension and tachycardia with rapid administration of the agent. Nitrous oxide can cause myocardial depression, but this effect is offset by the sympathetic increasing leading to minimal hemodynamic changes.

retrieved_docs.title: Desflurane -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Desflurane, along with the other inhalational agents, sevoflurane and isoflurane, is a potent vasodilator and can cause a decrease in blood pressure by decreasing systemic vascular resistance. A concomitant increase in heart rate sometimes occurs. Cardiac output is typically preserved with the use of this agent. Desflurane dilates cerebral arteries and decreases the cerebral metabolic rate.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia Inhalation Agents and Their Cardiovascular Effects -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Halogenated agents, including sevoflurane, desflurane, isoflurane, enflurane, and halothane, decrease mean arterial pressure in a dose-dependent manner as concentrations of the anesthetic gas increase. [3] [4] [3] The mean arterial pressure is reduced primarily due to decreased systemic vascular resistance, except for halothane, which lowers mean arterial pressure by directly affecting the myocardium and reducing cardiac output without altering systemic vascular resistance. [5] Sevoflurane has shown a lesser effect on hemodynamic and cardiovascular parameters compared to desflurane and isoflurane, contributing to lower morbidity and mortality. [6] Unlike other inhaled anesthetics, nitrous oxide does not affect mean arterial pressure. When combined with halogenated agents, nitrous oxide mitigates or even reverses mean arterial pressure reductions. [7] [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia Inhalation Agents and Their Cardiovascular Effects -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Over the past several decades, multiple studies and clinical practice have demonstrated the cardiovascular effects of inhalation anesthetic agents. Early anesthetics included diethyl ether and nitrous oxide, whereas more recent halogenated agents include isoflurane, desflurane, and sevoflurane. Halogenated agents produce similar circulatory effects in young, healthy individuals during maintenance anesthesia. [1] However, comorbidities, extremes of age, concurrent medications, and additional factors can alter the expected effects. The development of the anesthetic care team approach underscores the importance of effective communication to improve outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Aortic Stenosis
retrieved_docs.content: The most important aspect of anesthetic management of patients with aortic stenosis is maintaining afterload. Phenylephrine is the vasopressor of choice as it is a pure alpha agonist. Intravenous fluids can augment preload, but judicious use is advised as volume overload can result in pulmonary edema. Decreased systemic vascular resistance associated with neuraxial anesthesia should be expected and managed with phenylephrine to increase afterload. Tachycardia should be avoided to ensure adequate left ventricular filling. If aortic stenosis is severe, the aortic valve area is less than 0.3 cm and balloon valvuloplasty has been utilized with good outcomes. A pulmonary artery catheter should be utilized for patients with a transvalvular gradient greater than 50 mmHg. [1] [2] [7] Segmental epidural anesthesia is the anesthetic of choice but should be pursued with caution as decreases in preload and afterload may not be tolerated in this population. Slow titration of epidural anesthesia is ideal. Another option for the patient population is subarachnoid or intrathecal opioids. Spinal opioids have no cardiovascular effects as they preserve myocardial contractility, preload, and systemic vascular resistance. If general anesthesia is utilized, it should be approached with the same diligence as mitral stenosis patients, avoiding myocardial depression with volatile anesthetics. An opioid-based induction is beneficial when left ventricle function is not ideal. [1] [2] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia Inhalation Agents and Their Cardiovascular Effects -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Rapid increases in desflurane and isoflurane concentrations produce distinct effects on heart rate compared to maintenance levels. Desflurane elicits the most pronounced response. A rapid increase in desflurane concentration from 4% to 8% within 1 minute may cause heart rate and blood pressure to double above baseline in the absence of opioids, β-blockers, or clonidine. [14] This response is driven by a significant rise in sympathetic and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system activity. However, the effect diminishes after 30 minutes, suggesting receptor adaptation. Sevoflurane does not demonstrate this response during rapid concentration increases. A recent study found that heart rate remained stable despite epileptiform and generalized periodic discharges observed on electroencephalography. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia Inhalation Agents and Their Cardiovascular Effects -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Over the past several decades, extensive research has investigated the impact of inhalational anesthetics on cardiac output, heart rate, systemic vascular resistance, cardiac conduction, myocardial contractility, coronary blood flow, and blood pressure. These effects are dose-dependent and vary by agent. Halogenated agents, including isoflurane, desflurane, and sevoflurane, produce similar circulatory effects in young, healthy individuals during maintenance anesthesia. However, comorbidities, age extremes, concurrent medications, and other factors can alter these expected effects. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Intraoperative and Anesthesia Awareness -- Issues of Concern -- Patient-Related Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to acute hemodynamic compromise, patients presenting with chronic cardiovascular conditions such as congestive heart failure or significant valvular disease are also shown to be at higher risk for AAGA, as dose reduction strategies are commonly employed to maintain hemodynamic stability in these settings.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia Inhalation Agents and Their Cardiovascular Effects -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Sevoflurane -- Adverse Effects -- Cardiovascular
retrieved_docs.content: Sevoflurane induces a dose-dependent reduction in blood pressure and cardiac output primarily by reducing systemic vascular resistance.

retrieved_docs.title: Enflurane (Archived) -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Hemodynamic effects. The administration of enflurane has been known to decrease systemic vascular resistance. [7] This effect causes decreased blood pressure. Enflurane is also known to cause a concentration-dependent decrease in the heart and increased left atrial pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Management of Patients With Congenital Heart Disease -- Issues of Concern -- Anesthesia Induction and Maintenance
retrieved_docs.content: Mask induction is safe with volatile anesthetics in patients with less severe cardiorespiratory compromise. However, the duration of induction is significantly prolonged in cyanotic patients with reduced pulmonary blood flow. In this regard, the airway is partially controlled and may result in obstruction or hypoventilation, leading to hypoxemia. The inhalational agent of choice is sevoflurane. Due to the pungent odor of isoflurane and desflurane, anesthesia induction with these agents is relatively unsuitable.

retrieved_docs.title: Desflurane -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Rapid increases in desflurane concentration can cause a transient but clinically significant elevation in heart rate and blood pressure. These effects are secondary to catecholamine release, which is more pronounced with desflurane than isoflurane or sevoflurane. This resulting sympathetic response is controllable by concurrent administration of esmolol, clonidine, or the use of an opioid. Slowing the rate of increase in the concentration of desflurane can also decrease the catecholamine release. Volatile anesthetics promote skeletal muscle relaxation and enhance the effects of neuromuscular blocking agents. Desflurane enhances the effects of rocuronium greater than sevoflurane, isoflurane, or IV  anesthetics. As with all inhaled anesthetics, a decrease in the ventilatory response to CO 2 is expected. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Single Ventricle and Anesthesia Management -- Clinical Significance -- Intraoperative Management
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrous oxide is typically avoided, as it significantly increases PVR. Among intravenous agents, propofol is known to cause a marked reduction in SVR and cause myocardial depression, which can significantly reduce cardiac output and divert blood away from pulmonary circulation. [18] Etomidate is considered a safer option due to its minimal cardiovascular impact. [19] Ketamine can also be used in patients with preserved ventricular function, as it increases SVR through catecholamine release without significantly affecting PVR. Fentanyl and dexmedetomidine have minimal effects on myocardial contractility, SVR, and PVR, making them well-suited for use in this population. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Management of Ex Utero Intrapartum Treatment (EXIT) and Fetal Surgery -- Issues of Concern -- 3. Uterine Relaxation
retrieved_docs.content: Volatile anesthetic agent: Most of the volatile anesthetics depress the amplitude and frequency of the uterine contraction in a dose-dependent manner (Desflurane> sevoflurane> halothane> isoflurane). [4] Desflurane and sevoflurane have low blood-gas partition coefficients resulting in rapid onset and offset of action and are commonly used for tocolysis for fetal surgeries, especially when an adequate uterine tone is desired immediately after profound atony during the procedure. High minimum alveolar concentration (MAC) such as 2 to 2.5 of MAC of volatile anesthetics can give adequate tocolysis but at the expense of maternal hypotension, decreased fetal blood flow, fetal acidosis, and vasopressor requirements. The higher fetal concentration of these anesthetic agents is also associated with impaired systolic function and fetal cardiac depression. [5] [6] A lower concentration of gas (1 to 1.5 MAC) can be used with adequate relaxation when supplemented with IV anesthetics like propofol and remifentanil or when used with other tocolytics. [7] If deemed inadequate, nitroglycerine bolus or infusion may be added to achieve adequate uterine atony. The downside of using volatile anesthetic as tocolytic is the need for general anesthesia for its delivery, while many of these procedures can be carried out safely under regional anesthesia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2378
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a range of techniques used to assess cardiac function and hemodynamic status, particularly in the peri‑operative setting. It includes specific child procedures such as Monitoring of ECG at surgery and Monitoring of ECG, pressure, blood gases and cardiac output, which together provide continuous electrical, pressure, respiratory gas, and flow information. A central focus of cardiovascular monitoring is the percutaneous insertion of catheters into arterial sites for direct pressure and waveform acquisition; these sites include the left femoral artery, left temporal artery, right axillary artery, right carotid artery, and right radial artery. Each arterial catheterization allows real‑time assessment of arterial pressure and can be integrated with other monitoring modalities. Additionally, Transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring is classified as a type of cardiovascular monitoring, offering ultrasound‑based visualization of cardiac structures and function from the esophagus. Together, these interconnected monitoring strategies enable comprehensive evaluation of cardiovascular status, guide intra‑operative decision‑making, and support optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2789
retrieved_docs.content: Deslanosid is a pharmacologic agent that may be employed in the management of decreased cardiac output, indicating its potential therapeutic role in conditions where the heart’s pumping ability is compromised.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 931
retrieved_docs.content: Arterial cannulation with cardiac‑output measurement is classified as a form of other vascular catheterization that does not involve direct instrumentation of the heart and is performed by percutaneously inserting an arterial catheter for continuous hemodynamic monitoring. The procedure can be carried out in a wide range of arterial sites—including the left and right axillary arteries, left and right carotid arteries, left and right femoral arteries, left and right radial arteries, and left and right temporal arteries—providing routes for real‑time assessment of arterial pressure, flow, and cardiac performance. Within the broader domain of cardiovascular monitoring, clinicians integrate data from electrocardiogram (ECG) monitoring, arterial pressure recordings, blood‑gas analysis, and cardiac‑output measurements, and they may also employ transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring via an esophageal probe to obtain detailed ultrasound images of the heart and great vessels. Together, these invasive arterial catheterizations and imaging techniques enable comprehensive, continuous surveillance of circulatory function, guiding therapeutic decisions, enhancing patient safety, and improving outcomes in peri‑operative and critical‑care settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: The initial implantation of a cardiac single‑ chamber device is performed with the heart (srdce) as an indirect procedural site, whereas several intra‑operative monitoring techniques involve the heart directly. Monitoring of ECG at surgery, monitoring of ECG together with pressure, blood gases and cardiac output, and transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring each have the heart (srdce) as their direct procedure site. These relationships emphasize that both therapeutic device implantation and comprehensive cardiac monitoring rely on precise interaction with the heart, highlighting its central role in surgical care and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2298
retrieved_docs.content: Monitoring of ECG and pressure during major surgery, Monitoring of ECG, pressure, blood gases and cardiac output, Myocardial imaging, and Transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring are all directly associated with the heart (srdce) as their procedural site. These techniques illustrate how the heart serves as the focal point for intra‑operative hemodynamic monitoring, comprehensive cardiopulmonary assessment, and advanced imaging of myocardial structure and function. In addition, the heart (srdce) includes the concept of نفخة قلبية, referring to cardiac sounds or murmurs that are evaluated through auscultation. Together, these relationships underscore the central role of the heart in diverse diagnostic and monitoring modalities, highlighting the importance of integrated cardiac evaluation for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1128
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a broad range of specialized procedures aimed at continuously assessing cardiac function and related physiological parameters. It includes Electrocardiogram monitoring with a 10‑hour portrait protocol, Fetal electrocardiogram monitoring, and various intra‑operative monitoring modalities such as monitoring of ECG and pressure during major surgery, monitoring of ECG at surgery, and comprehensive monitoring of ECG, pressure, blood gases, and cardiac output. The focus of cardiovascular monitoring also extends to the percutaneous insertion of arterial catheters for real‑time data acquisition, with specific techniques described for catheter placement in the left femoral artery, left radial artery, left temporal artery, right axillary artery, and right temporal artery. Additionally, transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring is classified as a type of cardiovascular monitoring, providing detailed imaging of cardiac structures and function through the esophagus. Together, these interconnected monitoring strategies enable precise evaluation of cardiovascular status in diverse clinical settings, supporting informed decision‑making and optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2362
retrieved_docs.content: Vascular cannula insertion is a broad procedural category that includes the placement of a pulmonary artery catheter (قثطرة الشريان الرئوي) as one of its specific techniques. Pulmonary artery catheterization involves advancing a specialized cannula through a central vein into the pulmonary artery to obtain direct hemodynamic measurements such as pulmonary artery pressure, cardiac output, and mixed venous oxygen saturation. This subprocedure is commonly employed in critical care and perioperative settings to guide fluid management, assess cardiac function, and monitor patients with complex cardiopulmonary conditions.

retrieved_docs.content: cardioplegic electrolyte additives are integrated within a structured pharmacologic framework that supports fluid‑electrolyte management, organ protection, and therapeutic interventions in cardiac surgical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1572
retrieved_docs.content: Extracorporeal circulation auxiliary to open heart procedures is classified as شلّ القلب, indicating that this supportive technique used during open‑heart surgery is categorized under the concept of cardiac arrest.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 924
retrieved_docs.content: Arterial cannulation with cardiac output is classified as a type of other vascular catheterization and constitutes a core component of cardiovascular monitoring during major surgery. The procedure involves percutaneous insertion of arterial catheters for hemodynamic assessment into a range of specific sites—left and right axillary arteries, left and right carotid arteries, left and right femoral arteries, left and right radial arteries, and left and right temporal arteries. These arterial access points enable continuous monitoring of electrocardiogram (ECG), arterial pressure, blood gases, and cardiac output. Cardiovascular monitoring is described as a broader domain that also includes monitoring of ECG and pressure during major surgery, and it is linked to transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring, integrating invasive arterial data with echocardiographic evaluation of cardiac function. By selecting the most appropriate arterial site based on patient anatomy and clinical needs, clinicians can tailor monitoring strategies to improve the accuracy and comprehensiveness of cardiovascular surveillance in the peri‑operative setting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a range of specialized techniques used to assess cardiac function and related physiological parameters in various clinical settings. It includes specific monitoring modalities such as Electrocardiogram monitoring with a 10‑hour portrait, Fetal electrocardiogram, Monitoring of ECG and pressure during major surgery, Monitoring of ECG at surgery, and comprehensive Monitoring of ECG, pressure, blood gases, and cardiac output. The focus of cardiovascular monitoring also extends to the percutaneous insertion of arterial catheters for continuous measurement, with particular attention to catheter placement in the left carotid artery, left femoral artery, right radial artery, and right temporal artery. Additionally, Transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring is classified as a form of cardiovascular monitoring, providing detailed imaging of cardiac structures via the esophagus. Together, these interconnected methods enable precise real‑time evaluation of cardiac and hemodynamic status, supporting informed decision‑making and optimal patient care during surgical procedures, fetal assessment, and critical care management.

retrieved_docs.content: the generic procedure “procedure on artery” is linked inversely, indicating that general arterial procedures encompass these specialized techniques. Cardiovascular monitoring is organized as a parent domain that includes electrocardiogram monitoring (10‑hour portrait ECG and fetal ECG), ECG and pressure monitoring during major surgery, combined ECG‑pressure‑blood‑gas‑cardiac‑output monitoring, systemic arterial pressure monitoring, and transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring. Therapeutic arterial interventions (excluding the aorta) are linked to other vascular catheterization — not heart and to venous catheterization as related vascular‑access techniques. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 861
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a broad range of diagnostic and invasive techniques used to assess cardiac function and hemodynamics. It includes specific monitoring modalities such as Electrocardiogram monitoring with a 10‑hour portrait, Fetal electrocardiogram, Monitoring of ECG and pressure during major surgery, Monitoring of ECG at surgery, and comprehensive Monitoring of ECG, pressure, blood gases and cardiac output. In addition to these non‑invasive and semi‑invasive approaches, cardiovascular monitoring places particular emphasis on the percutaneous insertion of catheters for arterial pressure monitoring, targeting the left axillary artery, left femoral artery, left radial artery, right radial artery, and right temporal artery. These arterial catheterizations provide continuous waveform and pressure data essential for intra‑operative and critical‑care management. Furthermore, Transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring is classified as a type of cardiovascular monitoring, offering real‑time imaging of cardiac structures and function. Together, these interconnected methods form an integrated strategy for detailed cardiovascular assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1150
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a range of specialized procedures that assess cardiac function and related physiological parameters in various clinical contexts. It includes Electrocardiogram monitoring with a 10‑hour portrait protocol, Fetal electrocardiogram monitoring, and comprehensive intra‑operative monitoring such as ECG and pressure monitoring during major surgery, ECG monitoring at surgery, and combined monitoring of ECG, arterial pressure, blood gases, and cardiac output. The focus of cardiovascular monitoring also involves the percutaneous insertion of catheters into specific arterial sites for continuous measurement, namely the left axillary artery, left femoral artery, left temporal artery, right femoral artery, and right temporal artery. Additionally, transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring is classified as a type of cardiovascular monitoring, providing detailed imaging of cardiac structures via the esophagus. Together, these modalities and catheter‑based approaches constitute an integrated framework for real‑time assessment of cardiovascular status, guiding therapeutic decisions and enhancing patient safety during both routine and high‑risk procedures.
